dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3, priority 1 for a Stroke/CVA at VCU Sanger Hall.
Hi, I'm Paramedic Jordan, with ambulatory EMS. Welcome-what brought you to call 911 today?
My husband stood up off schedule, and I found him unconscious in the hallway.
Mr. Pritchard, can you open your eyes please? Hello, Mr. Pritchard, can you tell me your name?
Hmm... E-E-Eli.
Okay, Mr. Pritchard, what county are we in? Can you tell me where we are?
C-C-Chesterfield?
We're going to confirm your airway is clear, Mr. Pritchard. I'm going to look, listen, and feel.
Airway is patent. No obstruction. Patient is breathing on his own.
Respirations are 22 and regular with clear bilateral breath sounds. Look normal.
Mr. Pritchard, were you feeling any shortness of breath earlier?
Pulse is strong and regular at 85. Skin is cool, pink, and clammy with normal turgor.
Circulation appears normal at this time.
He was at work, and I noticed he hadn't come home. Went to find him and discovered him on the floor.
He wasn't conscious at first. VCU police and his son were there when I arrived.
Mr. Pritchard, can you tell me what symptoms you were experiencing earlier?
I felt dizzy, nauseous, and couldn't move my right arm. I don't remember much since 5 p.m.
On a scale from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst, can you rate the severity of your symptoms now?
It's mostly a 2... manageable mostly, but... confusing.
Skin is pink, cool, and clammy, no bruising or rashes noted. Normal turgor.
Mr. Pritchard, we are going to check your eyes.
Can you hold out your right and left arms now, Mr. Pritchard?
He has right side weakness compared to the left. Grip is poor.
Chest is symmetrical, no abnormal rise or fall. No tracheal deviation noted.
No abnormalities or pain noted in the pelvic area.
Oxygen saturation is 99%.
We are going to check your blood sugar now. Prepare for a small pinch.
Blood glucose is 121 mg/dL.
Blood pressure is 119 over 74.
Your breathing rate is 22 per minute-still normal.
Heart rate is 85, strong and regular.
Pupils are equal and reactive at 4 mm.
Abdomen is soft, non-tender, with no distention or guarding noted.
Performing 4 lead ECG now. Skin is ready, monitoring begun.
12-lead ECG obtained. Some T-wave abnormality noted per manual interpretation, still in sinus rhythm.
Per findings and GCS 14 (Eye 4, Verbal 4, Motor 6), we are proceeding with Stroke/TIA Protocol for transport.
Rechecking vitals after procedures-BP is 121/73, HR 76, and respirations 20 per minute.
SpO2 on room air is 100%. Condition appears stable at this time.
IV remains secure and flowing normally as established.
Mr. Pritchard, do you have any pain right now? Do you feel dizzy or sick?
Per your initial input and stroke diversion protocols in place, we're heading to Johnston-Willis.
Mr. Pritchard was initially diaphoretic and altered. He is now more alert and communicative.
Mr. Pritchard, we're on the way. Please speak up anytime. Your vitals are being monitored.
Stretcher one, prepare for ED Stroke Launch Pad at the hospital upon arrival.
Mr. Pritchard's wife and son followed in their car alongside us.
Mr. Pritchard vomited two times during transport.
Tests complete. Proceeding to ED Stroke Launch Pad as planned.
Mr. Pritchard, we positioned you to be more comfortable during transport-sitting upright with support.
Ready at ED. Any messages or extra details I should be aware of before patient arrives?
Mr. Pritchard had neurological symptoms and likely TIA. He is now alert with no deficits that we see.
Vitals and ECG are stable. Patient transported in no distress.
All patient information is here, including reports and time series.
Thank you. I'll be ready once you confirm arrival. Can you keep me updated en route?
We'll stay where you told us until ED is ready to take Mr. Pritchard.
Handover completed. Mr. Pritchard, hold on tightly.
Mr. Pritchard moved himself to the hospital bed upon arrival with some verbal guidance.
Patient secured, ready for ED care to begin.
As per notes and his wife, no medications, allergies, or surgeries reported.
Thanks for the clarification. Reports seem good. We'll take over from here.
Reports given verbally and in writing. Care transition initiated.
Feel free to reach out if clarification on treatment is needed.
EMS will stay until confirmed handover to the ED is complete.
Patient was moved with VCU PD present, no injury noted in process.
Patient transported with no observable additional issues.
Vitals and care reviewed. Thank you, team.
We're done. Cared for a patient with likely TIA.
Unit 3, sign off complete. All patient data received.
Unit 3 released, care transfer confirmed at J-W Hospital.
Mr. Pritchard still has no acute pain or new symptoms in the last assessment.
Recent vitals were within range, as initially reported.
BP at 121/73, HR 76. No new findings to note.
Procedures and documents are clear for ED staff to follow.
No deviations or extra notes beyond what is already in the report.
All steps done on time and with proper protocol.
Unit 3 logging out. All post-care activities confirmed.
ED assumed care at 22:44:59. All protocols followed.
Name, address, age, and gender captured in initial narrative.
Full process from start confirmed and recorded.
I'll monitor for any side effects or repeat symptoms.
Good team work with VCU PD and hospital. Job done.
Patient stayed calm and coherent, with no swelling or bruising noted.
Unit 3 signing off. Callcharts closed, gear cleaned and ready.
Call was standard EMS field work. All documented.
IV placement was successful with one attempt, transported stable.
Patient remained alert and verbal during transfer to hospital.
Handover notes were complete and pre-hospital care clear.
Transfer to J-W complete. All EMS actions aligned with hospital entry.
Right-sided weakness was reported and recorded for ED review.
All field findings, actions, and transfer provided with no gap.
Transport timing and condition reported consistent with field observation.
ED stroke care initiated with field care findings, as planned.
Pre-hospital report enhanced ED preparedness clearly.
Handover at J-W executed with no rework or adjustment.
Patient remains stable and alert as arrives and stays for ED care.
No further action required by EMS after hospital confirmation.
Dispatch time logged at 22:44, and ED readiness clear.
Care transition fast and without incident.
No additional problems noted from patient or ED upon arrival.
Unit 3 standing by for new calls or assignments.
Well done, team. All field reports valid and compliant.
